file_name: Zounds Hearing, Inc. - MANUFACTURING DESIGN MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Manufacturer shall provide to Subcontractor a purchase order setting forth the proposed quantity of Manufacturer's Products to be purchased by Manufacturer and delivery dates, which purchase order will be subject to written acceptance by Subcontractor. All purchase orders are non-cancellable, and the Manufacturer shall pay for the purchaser orders as follows: (i) for purchase orders for less than or equal to a total of five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of fifty percent (50%) of Product Cost (as defined below) shall be made in full at the time the order is placed and the remaining balance paid in full before the Manufacturer's Products are shipped; and (ii) for purchase orders for more than five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of one hundred percent (100%) of Product Cost shall be made in full at the time the order is placed.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer shall provide to Subcontractor a purchase order setting forth the proposed quantity of Manufacturer's Products to be purchased by Manufacturer and delivery dates, which purchase order will be subject to written acceptance by Subcontractor. Purchase orders are generally non-cancellable, however, cancellations may be considered under certain circumstances such as unforeseen economic downturn or changes in market demand. The Manufacturer shall pay for the purchaser orders as follows: (i) for purchase orders for less than or equal to a total of five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of fifty percent (50%) of Product Cost (as defined below) shall be made in full at the time the order is placed and the remaining balance paid in full before the Manufacturer's Products are shipped; and (ii) for purchase orders for more than five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of one hundred percent (100%) of Product Cost shall be made in full at the time the order is placed.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'All purchase orders are non-cancellable' is changed to 'Purchase orders are generally non-cancellable, however, cancellations may be considered under certain circumstances such as unforeseen economic downturn or changes in market demand.' This creates a contradiction with Section 8.1 which states 'All orders whether for Manufacturer's Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Manufacturer's Products are non-cancellable.' It is now unclear whether purchase orders can ever be cancelled.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Orders: All orders whether for Manufacturer's Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Manufacturer's Products are non-cancellable.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated 'All purchase orders are non-cancellable.' The changed text modifies this to 'Purchase orders are generally non-cancellable, however, cancellations may be considered under certain circumstances...'. The contradicted text states 'All orders whether for Manufacturer's Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Manufacturer's Products are non-cancellable.' The change introduces a contradiction because the changed text allows for cancellations under certain circumstances, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text that states all orders are non-cancellable without exception. This ambiguity about cancellability creates an in-text contradiction, falling under the definition of ambiguities in text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Manufacturer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are firm delivery dates.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are considered target delivery dates, with Subcontractor making reasonable efforts to meet them.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'all shipment dates are firm delivery dates', establishing a clear obligation. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that shipment dates are 'considered target delivery dates, with Subcontractor making reasonable efforts to meet them,' weakening the obligation. This creates a contradiction with section 15.2, which states that delays caused by the manufacturer shall not constitute cause for cancellation of Manufacturer's order, but shall extend Subcontractor's time to ship goods for a period equal to the duration of such contingency. Now the meaning of what will happen with delays caused by the subcontracted is not defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Except as otherwise provided herein, neither Party shall be liable to the other for its failure to perform any of its obligations hereunder during any period in which performance is delayed by circumstances beyond its reasonable control, including, without limitation, an act of God, war, civil disturbance, court order, labor dispute, third party nonperformance, acts of third parties, or failures, fluctuations or non-availability of materials, components, electrical power, heat, light, air conditioning, computing or information systems or telecommunications ("force majeure"), provided that the Party experiencing such delay promptly notifies the other Party of the delay and the cause thereof. The happening of any contingency beyond Subcontractor's reasonable control, including delays caused by Manufacturer or suppliers, shall not constitute cause for cancellation of Manufacturer's order, but shall extend Subcontractor's time to ship goods for a period equal to the duration of such contingency.
perturbation.justification: The original text established firm delivery dates, while the changed text weakens this to 'target delivery dates' with 'reasonable efforts.' This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text states that delays caused by the Manufacturer will extend the Subcontractor's time to ship, implying firm dates. However, the changed text now implies that the dates are merely targets, creating uncertainty and potentially conflicting with the 'extend Subcontractor's time to ship goods' provision.

perturbation.original_text: Manufacturer will be responsible for shipping costs.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer will typically be responsible for shipping costs, but the specific allocation will be negotiated on a per-shipment basis.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states unequivocally that 'Manufacturer will be responsible for shipping costs.' The revised text introduces uncertainty by stating 'Manufacturer will typically be responsible for shipping costs, but the specific allocation will be negotiated on a per-shipment basis.' This is contradictory to the original clear allocation of responsibility. This also creates an in-text contradiction of section 4, where states 'In addition to the purchase price, Manufacturer shall pay any and all transportation charges (including insurance).', because it no longer clear now that manufacturer will pay for the transportations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Delivery and Shipping. The agreed upon delivery dates are based on the Subcontractor's projected lead time, current inventory, commitments and Subcontractor's advice. Manufacturer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are firm delivery dates. All shipments shall be F.O.B. the manufacturing facility unless otherwise mutually agreed upon in writing. The method and route of shipment shall be at Subcontractor's discretion, unless Manufacturer supplies instructions in writing at least five days prior to shipment. In addition to the purchase price, Manufacturer shall pay any and all transportation charges (including insurance). The risk of loss of and title to the Manufacturer's Products pass to Manufacturer upon the receipt of the Manufacturer's Products by the carrier. Subcontractor is not responsible for any installation of Manufacturer's Products sold hereunder or delays caused by Manufacturer-specified suppliers.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the 'Manufacturer will be responsible for shipping costs.' The changed text introduces uncertainty by stating 'Manufacturer will typically be responsible for shipping costs, but the specific allocation will be negotiated on a per-shipment basis.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text which states, 'In addition to the purchase price, Manufacturer shall pay any and all transportation charges (including insurance).'. This removes the clear obligation and replaces it with a negotiable element, creating ambiguity about who ultimately pays for transportation, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
